title: Arrays & Loops
description: Vec<T> is Rust's dynamic array and the backbone of nearly every interview solution. Master push/pop, slice syntax, iterator chains, and 2D vectors to handle any array-based problem efficiently.

sections.title: Vec<T> basics and push/pop
sections.explanation: **Vec<T>** is Rust's growable array. Create with `vec![]` macro or `Vec::new()`. Use `.push()` to append and `.pop()` to remove the last element (returns Option<T>). Access elements with `nums[i]` (panics on out-of-bounds) or `.get(i)` (returns Option). `.len()` gives the size.

exercise.prompt: Create a Vec<i32> with values [10, 20, 30]. Push 40 and 50 onto it. Pop the last element and print it. Then print the length and the full vector.

validation.message: After push 40, 50 then pop: last=50, len=4, vec=[10,20,30,40]
Push 40 and 50, then pop the last element

exercise.hint: Push 40, then push 50. Pop removes the last pushed element (50).

sections.title: Slices and for loops
sections.explanation: A **slice** `&[T]` is a reference to a contiguous portion of a Vec. Use `&nums[start..end]` for a subslice. For loops iterate with `for x in &nums` (borrows) or `for x in nums.iter()`. Use `.enumerate()` to get `(index, value)` pairs — essential for interview problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a Vec with [5, 10, 15, 20, 25]. Print the slice from index 1 to 3 (exclusive). Then loop with enumerate and print each index:value pair space-separated.

validation.message: Slice [1..3] gives [10, 15]. Enumerate prints all index:value pairs.
Use &nums[1..3] for the slice and .enumerate() for index-value iteration

exercise.hint: Use &nums[1..3] to get elements at index 1 and 2. The range end is exclusive.

sections.title: Iterator methods (map, filter, collect)
sections.explanation: Rust iterators let you chain transformations fluently. Use `.iter()` to start, then `.map()`, `.filter()`, `.sum()`, `.collect()`, etc. This is idiomatic Rust and interviewers expect it. `.collect::<Vec<_>>()` materializes an iterator back into a Vec.

exercise.prompt: Given nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], use iterator methods to: (1) collect squares into a Vec, (2) filter only values > 3 and collect, (3) compute the sum. Print all three results.

validation.message: Use .map() for transformation, .filter() for selection, and .sum() for reduction

exercise.hint: Filter with **x > 3 (double deref because filter gives &&i32). Use .sum() for the total.

sections.title: 2D Vec (matrix/grid)
sections.explanation: For grid/matrix problems, use `vec![vec![0; cols]; rows]` to create a 2D Vec. Access with `grid[r][c]`. This is the standard pattern for DP tables, visited arrays, and adjacency matrices in interviews.

exercise.prompt: Create a 3x3 grid initialized to 0. Set the diagonal elements (0,0), (1,1), (2,2) to 1 (identity matrix). Print each row.

validation.message: Diagonal elements should be 1, all others 0
Set grid[i][i] = 1 for the diagonal and initialize with vec![vec![0; 3]; 3]

exercise.hint: Set grid[i][i] = 1 in the loop to place 1s on the diagonal.
